preamble: The shift from dental/alveolar [t] to the voiced postalveolar affricate [dʒ] is a significant development in the history of several languages, often mediated by palatalization and voicing.

examples.note: English yod-coalescence shows [t+j] moving toward [tʃ] and in some dialects or specific contexts, further voicing to [dʒ].
In several Romance varieties, dental stops followed by front vowels or glides underwent palatalization and affrication, eventually becoming voiced in some positions.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic.
